title: सुब्रह्मण्य कवचम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री सुब्रह्मण्य कवचम् ॥

ॐ अस्य श्री सुब्रह्मण्यकवचस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य ब्रह्मा ऋषिः।
गायत्री छन्दः। श्रीसुब्रह्मण्यो देवता।
ॐ बीजम्। श्रीं शक्तिः। सौं कीलकम्।

॥ ध्यानम् ॥
षडाननं कुङ्कुमरक्तवर्णं
महामतिं दिव्यमयूरवाहनम्।
रुद्रस्य सूनुं सुरसैन्यनाथं
गुहं सदाहं शरणं प्रपद्ये॥

॥ कवचम् ॥
शिरो मे शंकरसुतः पातु भालं षडाननः।
नेत्रे मे पातु सर्वज्ञः श्रोत्रे रक्षतु विश्वभुक्॥१॥

घ्राणं मे शक्तिधरः पातु मुखं पातु षडाननः।
जिह्वां पातु महासेनः कण्ठं मे शिखिवाहनः॥२॥

स्कन्दः पातु च मे हस्तौ हृदयं पातु गुहस्तथा।
कुमारः पातु मे नाभिं शरजन्मा च मे कटिम्॥३॥

ऊरू मे सेनापतिश्च जानू मे देवसेनापतिः।
जंघे मे विशाखः पातु पादौ मे शक्तिहस्तकः॥४॥

सर्वाङ्गं मे सदा पातु कार्तिकेयो महाबलः।
एतत्ते कवचं दिव्यं सर्वशत्रुनिवारणम्॥५॥

य इदं पठते नित्यं सुब्रह्मण्यस्य कवचम्।
तस्य शत्रुक्षयो नित्यं भवेदैश्वर्यमुत्तमम्॥६॥

रोगा नश्यन्ति सर्वे च भूतप्रेतभयं न च।
सर्वत्र विजयं तस्य यस्येदं कवचं करे॥७॥

॥ इति श्री सुब्रह्मण्य कवचम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Subrahmanya Kavacham ||

Om Asya Shri Subrahmanya-kavacha-stotra-mantrasya Brahma Rishih.
Gayatri Chhandah. Shri Subrahmanyo Devata.
Om Bijam. Shrim Shaktih. Saum Kilakam.

|| Dhyanam ||
Shad-ananam Kunkuma-rakta-varnam
Maha-matim Divya-mayura-vahanam.
Rudrasya Sunum Sura-sainya-natham
Guham Sadaham Sharanam prapadye.

|| Kavacham ||
Shiro me Shankara-sutah Patu Bhalam Shad-ananah.
Netre me patu Sarvajnah Shrotre Rakshatu Vishva-bhuk.||1||

Ghranam me Shakti-dharah patu Mukham patu Shad-ananah.
Jihvam patu Mahasenah Kantham me Shikhi-vahanah.||2||

Skandah patu cha me hastau Hridayam patu Guhastatha.
Kumarah patu me nabhim Shara-janma cha me katim.||3||

Uru me Senapatish-cha Janu me Deva-senapatih.
Janghe me Vishakhah patu Padau me Shakti-hastakah.||4||

Sarvangam me sada patu Kartikeyo Mahabalah.
Etat-te kavacham divyam Sarva-shatru-nivaranam.||5||

Ya idam pathate nityam Subrahmanyasya kavacham.
Tasya shatru-kshayo nityam bhaved-aishwaryam-uttamam.||6||

Roga nashyanti sarve cha Bhuta-preta-bhayam na cha.
Sarvatra vijayam tasya yasyedam kavacham kare.||7||

|| Iti Shri Subrahmanya Kavacham Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: सुब्रह्मण्य कवचम् में भगवान् कार्तिकेय के विभिन्न नामों से शरीर के अंगों की रक्षा का वर्णन है। शंकरसुत मस्तक की, षडानन ललाट की, सर्वज्ञ नेत्रों की, शक्तिधर नासिका की, महासेन जिह्वा की, शिखिवाहन कण्ठ की, स्कन्द हाथों की, गुह हृदय की, कुमार नाभि की और शरजन्मा कटि की रक्षा करें। इस दिव्य कवच के नित्य पाठ से शत्रुक्षय, ऐश्वर्य, सर्वरोगनाश और सर्वत्र विजय प्राप्त होती है।
benefits: Protects all body parts through Kartikeya's various names
Traditionally recited for removal of enemies and grants supreme prosperity
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of diseases and fear of evil spirits
Traditionally recited to invoke victory in all endeavors
description: Subrahmanya Kavacham — सुब्रह्मण्य कवचम् — is a divine armor prayer for protection offered to Lord Kartikeya. This sacred composition is sourced from Traditional and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to destroy enemies and grant supreme prosperity and ensure victory in all endeavors. It is also said to remove all diseases and fear of evil spirits. For best results, devotees chant this on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and during Skanda Shashti. Comprising 7 verses, it can be completed in approximately 8 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Subrahmanya Kavacham in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Subrahmanya Kavacham in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Divine armor of Lord Kartikeya for protection against enemies and diseases.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Subrahmanya Kavacham?
seoFaqs.answer: The Subrahmanya Kavacham (सुब्रह्मण्य कवचम्) is a classical Sanskrit protective-armor hymn dedicated to Lord Subrahmanya — also known as Skanda, Karttikeya, Murugan, Kumara, Guha, Shanmukha, Saravana Bhava and Velayudha — the six-faced commander of the celestial armies and son of Lord Shiva and Mata Parvati. The kavacham is preserved in classical Shaiva tradition and is widely recited in South Indian temples, by Karttikeya devotees in Bengal and Maharashtra, and increasingly across all India. Different textual versions circulate, including the famous Subrahmanya Bhujanga Stotram of Adi Shankaracharya which is often used as a kavacham, and shorter Sanskrit kavachas drawn from various Tantric and Puranic sources. (Sources: Subrahmanya Bhujanga Stotram of Adi Shankara; classical Skanda kavacha tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Lord Subrahmanya and what are his principal names and forms?
seoFaqs.answer: Lord Subrahmanya is the second son of Lord Shiva and Mata Parvati, born to slay the demon Tarakasura who could only be killed by a son of Shiva. According to the Skanda Purana, Shiva emanated six sparks of fire from his third eye which were carried by Agni to the river Ganga and deposited in the Sharavana reed forest, where six baby boys were born and nursed by the six Krittika nakshatra-mothers. Mata Parvati then embraced all six together, fusing them into one six-faced (Shanmukha), twelve-armed boy named Skanda. He has many names: Subrahmanya (the supremely Brahman-knowing); Karttikeya (the son of the Krittikas); Skanda (the leaper); Murugan (the eternal youth in Tamil); Kumara (the boy); Guha (the cave-dweller in the heart); Saravana (born in Sharavana); Velayudha (wielder of the vel); Shanmukha (six-faced); Tarakajit (slayer of Taraka); and Devasenapati (commander of the deva army). (Sources: Skanda Purana; Mahabharata Vana Parva; Tamil Murugan tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between the Subrahmanya Kavacham and the Skanda Sashti Kavacham?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are protective hymns of Lord Subrahmanya / Murugan but differ in language, composer, length and tradition. The Skanda Sashti Kavacham (Kanda Sashti Kavasam) is a 244-line Tamil kavacham composed in the 19th century by the Tamil saint-poet Devaraya Swamigal of Cuddalore, written in classical Tamil aciriya viruttam meter, and is the most widely chanted Murugan hymn in Tamil Nadu and the Tamil diaspora. The Subrahmanya Kavacham, by contrast, is a Sanskrit kavacham (with multiple textual versions including the Bhujanga Stotram of Adi Shankara), preserved in Sanskrit Shaiva tradition, recited especially in South Indian Brahmin households, Karttikeya temples of Bengal and Maharashtra, and pan-Indian Sanskrit recitation circles. They share the same iconography and mantras (Saravana Bhava, Vel) but address different language-communities and sampradayas. (Sources: Kanda Sashti Kavasam; Subrahmanya Bhujanga Stotram; classical kavacha tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Saravana Bhava mantra of Lord Subrahmanya?
seoFaqs.answer: Saravana Bhava — the six-syllable mula mantra of Lord Subrahmanya (Sa-Ra-Va-Na-Bha-Va) — is the supreme mantra of Murugan worship, corresponding to his six faces, his birth in the Sharavana reed forest, his nurturing by the six Krittika mothers, and his cosmic identity as Shanmukha. Each of the six syllables is identified with one of the six chakras of the yogic body and one of the six Krittika nakshatra mothers. The expanded form Om Saravana Bhavaya Namaha is the most commonly chanted Murugan mantra worldwide, while initiated devotees receive the bija mantra forms (Saum, Sham) from a guru. The Saravana Bhava mantra appears repeatedly in the Subrahmanya Kavacham as the core protective formula and is considered especially powerful for destroying inner demons, fears, and evil influences. (Sources: Subrahmanya Bhujanga Stotram; classical Murugan mantra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Subrahmanya Kavacham be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Subrahmanya Kavacham is most powerful when recited on Tuesdays (Mangalvar — sacred to Mars, the planet associated with Murugan), Fridays (Sashti Vrat days), every Sashti tithi (the sixth lunar day), Krittika nakshatra days, Vishakha nakshatra days, and during the great Murugan festivals: Skanda Sashti (Aippasi Shukla Sashti), Thaipusam (Tamil month of Thai, Pushya nakshatra), Vaikasi Visakam (Vaisakha Visakha — Murugan Jayanti), and Panguni Uttiram (his marriage day). The recitation should be at Brahma muhurta or before sunrise, after a bath, facing east or south (Murugan being the south-facing deity), on a clean asana, ideally before a picture or murti of Murugan with his peacock and vel. Many devotees observe a 48-day mandala parayana with daily recitation, or six recitations during the six days of Skanda Sashti. (Sources: classical Murugan paddhati; Subrahmanya kavacha tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Subrahmanya Kavacham bestow according to tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti and classical commentaries enumerate manifold benefits of regular Subrahmanya Kavacham recitation: complete protection from all enemies, both human adversaries and the inner enemies of kama, krodha, lobha, moha, mada and matsarya; destruction of evil spirits, ghosts, drishti dosha and black magic; pacification of the planet Mars (Mangal) in the natal chart, especially powerful for resolving Manglik dosha and marriage delays; cure of skin diseases, blood disorders and pitta-related ailments (Mars governing rakta and pitta); protection of children from disease and asura-baadha; success in competitive examinations, sports, the armed forces and any field requiring courage; cultivation of brahmacharya, valor, intelligence and dharma-vichara; pacification of unjust litigation; and the supreme phala of Murugan-bhakti and liberation through the grace of Saravana Bhava. (Sources: classical Subrahmanya kavacha phalashruti; Murugan-bhakti tradition.)
